title: FDA approval puts Wockhardt’s Zaynich in $9 billion antibiotics market
summary: Wockhardt's new antibiotic, Zaynich, has received US FDA approval. This drug uses a novel mechanism to fight drug-resistant bacteria. It targets superbugs that resist current treatments. Zaynich offers a powerful synergy to overcome resistance. The drug has a...
thbAction: Position THB's pharma CRM and omnichannel HCP engagement workflows to support launch planning, KAM orchestration, and compliant follow-up across India and the Middle East.
contentHtml:
Wockhardt’s Zaynich, which has got the US FDA approval for treating complicated urinary tract infections (cUTI), has the potential to redefine the fight against drug-resistant pathogens and infectious diseases, says Mahesh Patel, the brain behind the drug.

Zaynich (cefepime + zidebactam) has a “beta-lactam enhancer mechanism” that can simultaneously block two sequential steps in a bacterial pathway and works on superbugs that show resistance against existing antibiotic treatments, Patel, chief mentor, drug discovery at Wockhardt, told ET. “The synergy generated is so powerful that it overcomes virtually all known resistance mechanisms.”

Also Read: Wockhardt secures US FDA approval for antibiotic Zaynich

The drug has an estimated market opportunity of about $9 billion. In the US alone, cUTI is responsible for over 600,000 hospitalisations annually. Besides, more than 2.8 million antimicrobial resistant (AMR) infections occur each year in the US, resulting in more than 35,000 deaths.

Wockhardt expects US market exclusivity for Zaynich to extend to approximately 2038, Patel told ET’s Vikas Dandekar and Rica Bhattacharyya in an interview. Edited excerpts:

How significant is the US FDA approval for Zaynich?

It is a big achievement for Wockhardt. The true value is that this drug brings a completely new science in the management of infectious diseases. Its mechanism of action is what we call the beta-lactam enhancer mechanism.

People are familiar with beta-lactamase inhibitors, a concept that has existed for over 45 years and given rise to drugs such as Augmentin. What we have done is fundamentally different.

The inspiration came from an old drug, Bactrim (sulfamethoxazole + trimethoprim), approved in 1969. The power of that combination came from inhibiting two different biochemical steps in folic acid synthesis, creating a potent synergy, and thereby overcoming existing resistance.

We asked whether the same principle could be applied to bacterial cell wall synthesis, which is also a multi-step biochemical process. That idea stayed with me from my early research days and eventually became the foundation of zidebactam.

How does zidebactam work?

Zidebactam is a unique molecule. Like penicillin, cephalosporins and carbapenems, it is a cell-wall synthesis inhibitor. However, chemically it is not a beta-lactam. It belongs to a class called diazabicyclooctane, or DBO. What we discovered is that zidebactam binds very effectively to penicillin-binding protein 2 (PBPs are bacterial enzymes essential for building the cell wall), a critical protein responsible for maintaining the rod-shaped structure of Gram-negative bacteria (pathogens that are difficult to kill).

Also Read: US FDA review boosts odds for Wockhardt’s NCE success story: Vishal Manchanda

Nature has conserved this protein across major gram-negative pathogens such as E coli, Klebsiella, Pseudomonas and Acinetobacter. Zidebactam binds to PBP2 across these pathogens.

We then combined zidebactam with cefepime, which binds to PBP3. By simultaneously blocking PBP2 and PBP3, we inhibit two sequential steps in bacterial cell-wall synthesis. The synergy generated is so powerful that it overcomes virtually all known resistance mechanisms.

Unlike betalactamase inhibitors, we are not trying to inhibit individual resistance enzymes. There are more than 8,000 beta-lactamases known today. A single inhibitor cannot realistically cover all of them. Our approach bypasses that limitation.

You describe zidebactam as a beta-lactam enhancer rather than a beta-lactamase inhibitor. What is the distinction?

This is an important distinction. The concept comes from biochemical pathway inhibition. When you inhibit two sequential steps in a pathway, you create synergy. That is what happened with Bactrim decades ago and that is what happens in this drug as well.

The world was largely focused on beta-lactamase inhibitors. We took a different route. We realised very early that inhibitor-based approaches would only provide incremental improvements because bacteria possess thousands of different beta-lactamases.

Instead, we focused on complementary PBP binding. By inhibiting PBP2 and PBP3 together, bacterial cells undergo rapid death irrespective of which beta-lactamase enzymes they produce.

(Note: Betalactams such as penicillin and cephalosporins are drugs used to kill bacteria while betalactamase are enzymes produced by the bacteria to destroy those drugs.)

Looking back, what were the biggest milestones in the journey to the drug’s approval?

Zidebactam was born on August 16, 2011, when our medicinal chemistry team successfully synthesised the molecule for the first time. It is an extraordinarily challenging molecule to make, requiring around 17-18 synthetic steps. Our first US FDA interaction occurred in 2012, focused on designing the toxicology package. We filed our US IND around 2014-15 and then completed extensive Phase I studies. At that stage, the FDA was sufficiently impressed by the data. They granted us a waiver from conducting a Phase II efficacy programme, allowing us to move directly into Phase III. Another major challenge emerged when the Russia-Ukraine war began. Many of our original clinical sites were in Ukraine. We had already trained investigators and supplied study materials. When the war started, we had to relocate sites across Eastern Europe, India and China, delaying the programme by almost a year.

How long will Wockhardt enjoy market exclusivity?

Our basic patent protection extends until 2032. In addition, we expect patent term extensions and other regulatory exclusivities. We will also undertake paediatric development, which can provide a further six months of exclusivity. Overall, we expect US exclusivity to extend to approximately 2038, with similar protection available in Europe. Unfortunately, India doesn't have this kind of provision in intellectual property rights (IPR).

What comes next in Wockhardt’s pipeline?

We have built a beta-lactam enhancer platform. The next major programme is WCK 6777, which combines zidebactam with ertapenem instead of cefepime. The advantage is convenience. Cefepime requires administration three times daily, whereas ertapenem is a once-daily drug. This aligns well with the growing trend toward Outpatient Parenteral Antimicrobial Therapy (OPAT), where patients can receive treatment at home or in infusion centres without prolonged hospitalisation. The US National Institutes of Health conducted the Phase I study. The programme is expected to enter Phase II shortly. We also have an oral beta-lactam enhancer programme in development and expect to file a US IND within about a year.

As antimicrobial resistance (AMR) continues to evolve, how well-positioned is Wockhardt’s pipeline to address newer threats?

While we were working on Zaynich during the last four or five years, a completely new resistance mechanism emerged, particularly in E coli. But Zaynich overcomes this mechanism because of its combined action. We were among the earliest groups globally to recognise this emerging resistance mechanism and found that Zaynich remained highly effective. The same advantage will continue with WCK 6777. And then there is nafithromycin (Miqnaf), which treats community-acquired bacterial pneumonia in just three days. If you combine all these drugs together, our pipeline covers more than 90-95% of AMR challenges, across community, outpatient ward and ICU settings.

What does the global competitive landscape look like for beta-lactam enhancers?

We are pioneers in this field. In our assessment, zidebactam is significantly more potent than other research candidates and we believe we have established a unique position globally.

Beyond cUTI, where else could Zaynich be used for?

There are four or five major indications. The first is hospital-acquired pneumonia (HAP) and ventilator-associated pneumonia (VAP). These are among the most life-threatening hospital infections globally, with mortality rates often around 30%. We already have US FDA agreement on the clinical protocol for this programme. The study will involve more than 100 clinical sites globally and is expected to take around three years. The second indication is complicated intra-abdominal infections. The third is bloodstream infections, or bacteraemia, particularly those caused by Gram-negative pathogens.

We have also seen encouraging results in bone and joint infections through compassionate use programmes. These infections are notoriously difficult to treat because of poor drug penetration into bone and the formation of bacterial biofilms. Among roughly 85-90 compassionate-use patients treated globally, around 30-35 had bone and joint infections, with successful outcomes. It is not possible to do all the indications. But we are still going to do this HAP (hospital acquired pneumonia) and VAP (ventilator associated pneumonia). We have planned that study and it will start within a few months.

How meaningful could Zaynich be in ICU settings?

ICUs are the real red-hot zone for antimicrobial resistance. Today, many patients are treated with older drugs such as colistin or polymyxins, which are often toxic and relatively ineffective. Acute kidney injury is common, and many patients ultimately require dialysis. Zaynich provides a safe, life-saving alternative. Earlier access could make a significant difference to outcomes because it addresses resistance while avoiding the toxicity associated with many current salvage therapies.

Why was meropenem (a powerful, broad-spectrum carbapenem antibiotic used in hospitals to treat severe and complicated bacterial infections) chosen as the comparator in clinical trials?

The FDA wanted a comparator that represented the gold standard of care. For many years, that has been meropenem. The study enrolled ~530 patients across nearly 10 countries, including India, China, Mexico, several European countries, and the United States. Patients were randomised in a 2:1 ratio, with two patients receiving Zaynich for every one patient receiving meropenem. Complicated urinary tract infection (cUTI) is the preferred first indication for many antibiotics because both infection and treatment response can be measured very clearly. The FDA also required a “test-of-cure” visit after treatment ended to demonstrate sustained bacterial eradication rather than temporary suppression. Importantly, this was a double-blind study. Regulators insist that patients receive potentially equivalent treatment. That is why comparing against a weaker therapy would not have been acceptable.
